Effect of cold rolling and subsequent heat treatment on microstructural
evolution and mechanical properties of Fe-Mn-Al-C-(Ni) based austenitic
low-density steels
* Corresponding author.
**Corresponding author. Defence Metalurgical Research Laboratory, Hyderabad, 5058, India.
EmalshviaBis)ailR)vjegaiih)e
(D. Kumar), amritendu@gmail.com(A. Mukhopadhyay), mahu.vemuri@dmrl.drdo. V. Madhu), mjnvprasad@itb.aci (M.J.N.V. Prasa).

1. Introduction
AR TI C LE I N F O
A B S T R A C T
Materials Science & Engineering A 861 (2022) 144324
Thermo-mechanical processing in a two-phase domain of low-density steel (LDs) has the potential of enhancing
strength-ductility combination. In the present study, the effects of alloy composition, cold rolling and heat
treatment temperature (973–1173 K) on concurrent precipitation of κ-carbide/B2 particles and recrystallization
and subsequently, mechanical properties include hardness variation and tensile response are evaluated. In order
to understand the synergistic effect of
ratio towards selecting the two-phase processing window, three
LDS alloy compositions, namely Fe-28Mn–9Al-0.9C, Fe-28Mn–9Al1-0.9C-5Ni and Fe-15Mn–9Al-0.9C-5Ni (in
weight percent) are vacuum induction melted, hot rolled and then cold rolled to
reduction in thickness at
room temperature. The LDS aloys containing Ni showed much delayed recrystallization as compared to Ni-free
LDS. The
ratio played a vital role towards controlling precipitation and recrystalization and accordingly,
the mechanical properties. Al the three alloys exhibited similar hardness, strength and ductility levels after
(cid:)
cold reduction. Upon heat treatment, there was precipitation of intergranular and intragranular B2 phase par-
ticles along with
-carbides, whose fraction decreased with increasing the temperature. The tensile response of
LDS alloys was influenced primarily by the volume fraction of these precipitates as well as the recrystallized
austenite grains. Planar slip activity, dynamic slip band refinement in conjunction with dislocation-twin inter-
action mechanisms were noticed in fully recrystallized austenite and thereby leading to enhanced strain hard-
ening and ductility. Furthermore, lowering Mn in Ni containing LDS reduced the processing temperature window
of the alloy in two-phase region.
Austenite based low-density steel (LDs) is one of the advanced high
strength steels and is considered as a potential candidate material for
certain automotive and defense applications owing to its high specific
strength and ductility combinations. These alloys typically have high Mn
and C content to stabilize austenite phase at room temperature. Apart
from inducing significant hardening by solid solution and facilitating
intermetallic precipitation, aluminum (Al) addition is known to reduce
physical density of steel significantly [1–4]. Further, nickel (Ni) addition
to Fe–Mn–Al–C based alloy system leads to formation of hard interme-
tallic particles such as
(B2) and
, and thereby
attain superior strength in LDS alloys [5–10]. Based on alloying and
processing conditions, Fe-(25–30)Mn-(8–11)Al-(0.8–1.2)C-(0–5)Ni (all
elements in wt.
) based LDS alloys show triplex structure with
austenite
, ferrite (disordered/ordered B2) and kappa (κ) carbides as
main microstructural constituents [4–10]. Most of these alloys are re-
ported to be processed at temperatures higher than
having
completely recrystallized austenite as predominant microstructure,
resulting into lower yield strengths. Strength enhancement is achieved

2.1. Thermo-mechanical processing
2. Materials and experimental methods
B. Mishra et al.
Hardness and tensile tests of heat-treated samples were conducted to
evaluate the change in mechanical properties for the chosen alloys upon
cold working and heat treatment. The bulk hardness was measured as
per ASTM E–140-02 using an AFFRI Vickers hardness tester under a
peak load of
(cid:)
for a dwell time of
Hardness values
averaged over ten measurements on each sample were reported. Flat
tensile specimens with a gauge length of
,width
and
thickness
were prepared and tested as per ASTM E8M standard
for evaluating strength and ductility of the chosen alloys in different
processing conditions. Tensile tests were conducted at room tempera-
ture under a constant cross-head speed of
(initial strain
rate of
using Walter
testing machine. The tensile
tests were triplicated for each condition and the average of three read-
ings was reported. Additionally, interrupted tensile tests were carried
out on selected specimens to understand the role of microstructure in
general and second phase in particular on evolution of microstructure
with progress of deformation.
chemical analysis and emission spectroscopy is provided in Table 1. The
sound portion of the

This processing condition is hereafter
referred to as HR. The final thickness of hot rolled plate was about 13
mm. The details of the hot rolling process were reported elsewhere [21].
Samples were cut from the hot-rolled plates and were subsequently cold
rolled to
reduction in thickness
thick sheets) by sub-
jecting to several passes. It is important to note that 15Mn5Ni alloy has
experienced cracking beyond
cold reduction; hence, further
reduction was not carried out on any of the chosen alloys for comparison
reasons. The samples after cold rolling are denoted with
Subsequently, the cold rolled sheets were heat treated in the tempera-
ture range of 873 K–1273 K for
followed by water quenching. The
heat-treated samples are henceforth referred with the heat treatment
temperature.
in fully recrystallized alloys by subsequent ageing at 723 K–923 K
[11–14]. However, a prolonged ageing treatment reduces ductility of
LDS alloys due to precipitation of intergranular
-carbides [15,16]. It is
also reported that concurrent x-carbides and ferrite precipitation affects
the recrystallization kinetics. This phenomenon has been exploited by
several researchers to control the primary recrystallization of austenite
in Ni-free LDS for achieving better strength-ductility combination
[17–20]. Further, Ni addition in Fe-Mn–Al–C based LDS facilitates
precipitation of finer Ni-rich ordered B2 particles in the temperature
range of 973 K–1173 K [7–10]. It seems to be that Ni addition provides
an opportunity to further exploit thermo-mechanical processing window
to achieve better properties by fine tuning the recrystallization process
after cold deformation, which is not yet explored.
Orientation imaging microscopy (OIM) was performed on cold rolled
and heat-treated samples using Zeiss Supra 55 FEG-Scanning electron
microscope (SEM) equipped with EDS and Nordley EBSD detectors from
Oxford. Samples for OIM were polished to colloidal silica of
surface finish. A step size of
and
was employed on an
area of
and
for fine- and coarse-
grained samples, respectively. Three scans for each sample were used
to analyze the microstructure. The collated data were then analyzed
using TSL-OIMTm software for grain orientations, grain size and twin
fractions. Detailed analysis includes kernel average misorientation
(KAM) representing strain localization and grain orientation spread
(GOS) of
criterion to delineate the recrystallized microstructure.
Grain size of the recrystallized grains was determined using linear
intercept method. To calculate the volume fraction of twins, coherent
site lattice (CSL) Σ3 boundaries were selected. Based on the fraction of
Σ3 boundaries, twin fraction was reported. In addition, X-ray diffraction
studies were carried out on selected samples using
radiation at 45
kV and
with a beam size of
in a Rigaku XRD instrument.
Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) studies were carried out to
understand and identify the phases present in the heat-treated samples.
The specimens for TEM examination were prepared by twin jet electro-
polishing technique using mixed acid solution as electrolyte at
(cid:)
. A FEI TECNAI 20T G2 transmission electron microscope
operating at
equipped with EDAX energy dispersive X-ray
spectroscopy was used for TEM study. Post tensile fractography studies
were performed on selected fractured specimens of the alloys using Zeiss
EVO 18 SEM.
Zhang et al. [17] utilized concurrent second phase precipitation and
controlled recrystallization in Fe-26Mn-10.6Al-1.2C alloy to attain
better strength-ductility combination. A processing window was
explored by varying deformation (rolling reduction at room tempera-
ture) and subsequently, the annealing temperature and time. To opti-
mize the recrystallization kinetics thickness reduction levels of
and
annealing temperature window of 1123 K–1173 K were explored. The
best strength-ductility combination was reported for annealing tem-
perature and time combination of
and
, respectively [17]. On
similar lines, synergistic effect of partial recrystallization and
-carbide
strengthening resulting into higher yield strength and sufficient ductility
in Ni-free LDS was reported by annealing for shorter duration of time (3
min) at
[18]. Brasche et al. [19] reported change in
morphology and precipitation of
-carbides in deformed and recrystal-
lized austenite regions. Also, their interplay with recrystallization was
utilized to attain higher strength-ductility combination in Ni-free LDS. It
was also reported that Ni containing LDS shows fine ordered B2 pre-
cipitation during thermo-mechanical processing [7–10]. Similar to
K-carbide, concurrent B2 precipitation can be exploited for obtaining
superior properties by controlling the recrystallization process post cold
rolling deformation, which is not systematically addressed in literature.
In view of the gaps in literature, as mentioned above, the present
work is aimed to explore the effect of Ni addition vis-à-vis thermo-
mechanical processing on microstructure evolution and their effects
on mechanical properties include hardness and tensile response. A
comparison study has been made with Ni-free LDS. In this context, three
different compositions Fe–28Mn–9Al-0.9C, Fe-28Mn–9Al-0.9C–5Ni and
Fe-15Mn–9Al-0.9C–15Mn (all elements are in wt.
namely,
28Mn5Ni and 15Mn5Ni, respectively were investigated to understand
the effects of Mn and Ni on secondary phase precipitation and recrys-
tallization of austenite and subsequently on mechanical behavior of LDS
alloys.
For the present study, three low-density steel grades with different
Mn and Ni content were prepared by vacuum induction melting route.

3.1. Microstructural evolution
B. Mishra et al.

Microstructure evolution in all the three alloys under various heat
treatment conditions was examined in-detail and the results are pre-
sented in Figs. 3–7. Three main characteristic microstructural features
were observed in the heat-treated samples, namely (a) deformed
austenite, (b) recrystallized austenite along with annealing twins and (c)
secondary B2 phase. Two different types of B2 precipitation were
noticed, namely fine intragranular B2 (size:
and coarser
intergranular B2 (size:
). The former ones could not be
indexed with SEM-EBSD characterization. In addition, B2 stringers were
present prior to heat treatment in Ni containing low-density steels.
Microstructures of all the three alloys under investigation in hot
rolled (HR) condition were already reported elsewhere [21]. Summary
of the findings include: (i) all the three alloys exhibited equiaxed and
similar grain sizes of
-matrix
with nearly random texture
in HR condition; (ii) Ni addition led to formation of considerable
amounts of B2 phase in the form of elongated stringers (bamboo-like
morphology) and polygonal particles in
-matrix, where15Mn5Ni alloy
contained higher B2 fraction
than that of in 28Mn5Ni
(iii) similarly, 15Mn5Ni alloy possessed higher volume fraction of
-carbides followed by 28Mn5Ni and
alloys, and (iv) the mi-
crostructures of higher Mn LDS alloys consisted of higher fraction of
twins
than in 15Mn5Ni alloy.

It is also interesting to notice prominent coarser
precipitate (B2) rich zone around deformed austenite in 15Mn5Ni alloy,
whereas in 28Mn5Ni alloy fine secondary B2 was precipitated mostly
along grain boundaries. In addition, the inset in Fig. 4f shows the colored
IPF map for B2 phase alone, and it illustrates the presence of both prior
B2 phase and new B2 precipitates formed upon heat treatment at 1173
K. The prior B2 phase grains, which deformed heavily along with
-matrix in cold rolling, were fully recovered and developed bamboo-
type grain morphology upon heat treatment. Interestingly, the new B2
precipitates showed single crystal type orientations, but displayed some
recrystallization at
to complete recrystallization at
fol-
lowed by significant grain growth at

3.2. Mechanical behavior
3.2.1. Hardness variation
Materials Science & Engineering A 861 (2022) 144324
B. Mishra et al.
B. Mishra et al.
In order to characterize the deformed austenite regions and finer
microstructural features such as
-carbides and B2 particles which were
not revealed at SEM-EBSD scale, TEM studies were carried out on
selected heat-treated samples. Fig. 6a–d, e-f and g-h show the TEM im-
ages of 28Mn0Ni, 28Mn5Ni and 15Mn5Ni alloy samples heat treated at
973 K. Fig.
are dark-field TEM micrographs and the rest are
bright-field images. The dark-field images were captured from (010)
satellite spots corresponding to
-carbide in selected area electron
diffraction (SAED) patterns shown as insets in Fig. 6a–d. As noticed in
EBSD examination (Fig. 4), there were mixed regions of recrystallized
and deformed austenite grains in all the alloy samples heat treated at
In recrystallized austenite regions, fine uniformly distributed
intragranular
-carbides were seen (shown by dotted (orange) arrow in
Fig. 6b), while the deformed austenite region contained inhomogeneous
and relatively coarse

(Fig. 6d). The size of

15Mn5Ni alloy than that compared to in
and 28Mn5Ni alloys
under similar conditions. The observations of coarser and higher volume
fraction of
-carbides in deformed austenite and finer ones in recrys-
tallized region are consistent with earlier studies shown in Ni-free LDS
[17–19]. In addition to
-carbides, intergranular B2 particles were
observed in
alloy samples heat treated at
One such
region marking presence of intergranular B2 particles identified by solid
(white) arrow is shown in Fig. 6a. Similarly, fine intragranular B2 (5–20
nm sizes) marked by broken (yellow) arrow, intergranular B2 (50–200
nm sizes) particles as well as K-carbides were noticed in both 28Mn5Ni
(Fig. 6e and f) and 15Mn5Ni (Fig. 6g and h) alloy samples heat-treated at
973 K. The microstructure evolution in all the three alloys under heat
treatment at higher temperature of 1173 K is shown in Fig. 7. It is noted
that no secondary phase precipitates were seen in 28MnoNi alloy
(Fig. 7a and b). In case of Ni containing steels heat-treated at
(cid:)
fraction of intragranular B2 precipitation decreased with increase in
recrystallized austenite fraction (Fig. 7c–f). This indicates that highly
strained regions are preferential nucleation sites for these precipitates.

Fig. 8b illustrates the effect of subsequent heat treatment tempera-
ture on evolution of hardness in all the three alloys subjected to
cold reduction. The significant findings are as follows: (i) Upon subse-
quent heat treatment at
the hardness of Ni-containing alloys
(28Mn5Ni and 15Mn5Ni) was increased to
, while there was no
noticeable change in hardness of 28MnONi alloy. (ii) Under a given heat
treatment, hardness of Ni containing alloys was significantly higher than
that of 28Mn0Ni alloy, (iii) both the Ni containing alloys showed almost
similar trend in drop in hardness with the heat treatment temperature,
and (iv) the drop-in hardness with temperature was more prominent in
Ni-free
alloy. The variation in hardness of LDS alloys as a
function of heat treatment temperature could be related to substantial
and differential microstructural changes, as noted in previous section,
such as precipitation and dissolution of secondary phases (k-carbides
and B2 particles), recrystallization and grain growth phenomena etc.,
occurring in these alloys depending on the composition of steel and is

dislocation multiplication leading to planar slip activity followed by slip
band refinement with the applied strain in the fully recrystallized and
precipitate-free austenite grains of all the alloys. In the deformed
austenite regions of partially recrystallized sample
fine cross slip activity resulting into formation of dislocation cell

containing coarser and fine

noticed as obstacles to dislocation motion and thereby early initiation of

was developed in Ni containing LDS alloys, whereas Ni-free LDS

As mentioned in previous section, 15Mn5Ni alloy specimens heat
treated at
and 1073 K displayed negligible ductility
whereas
and 28Mn5Ni alloy specimens heat treated to the
same conditions showed remarkable tensile elongations. Hence, frac-
tography of all the three alloy tensile specimens heat treated at 1023 K
but failed in tension at room temperature is presented in Fig. 10a-c,
respectively. The morphology of fracture surface of 15Mn5Ni alloy
specimen (Fig. 10c) was distinctive from that of the other two alloy
specimens (Fig. 10a and b) and is in accordance with the tensile response
of the alloys. Both 28Mn0Ni and 28Mn5Ni alloys exhibited dimple
morphology whereas quasi-cleavage fracture was seen in 15Mn5Ni alloy
specimen. This indicates that the failure in both 28Mn0Ni and 28Mn5Ni
alloys was ductile in nature. Fracture in 15Mn5Ni alloy was seemed to
crack initiation-controlled with fast propagation of cracks. Large
numbers of microcracks were seen on fracture surface of 15Mn5Ni alloy
specimen. It is to be noted that 15Mn5Ni alloy showed much lesser
recrystallization of austenite matrix and large volume fraction of
secondary phase precipitation along grain boundaries forming precipi-
tate rich zones. Further, cross section of the failed tensile specimen of
15Mn5Ni alloy heat treated at
was examined in-detail. Fig. 10d
shows a scanning electron micrograph of one such crack regions
revealing presence of intergranular cracks. Fig. 10e and f shows the
OIM-IPF and phase maps, respectively of another cracked region, clearly
indicating propagation of crack along
interphase boundaries.

4.1. Role of alloy composition on concurrent precipitation and
recrystallization
Materials Science & Engineering A 861 (2022) 144324
B. Mishra et al.
B. Mishra et al.
Recently, some researchers have attempted to enhance the strength-
ductility tradeoff in Ni-free LDS by processing the material at lower
temperature range (873 K–1073 K) and thus controlling the interplay
between
-carbide size/fraction and recovery/recrystallization process
[18, 19]. This was in contrast to the earlier studies where strength
enhancement in LDs alloys was attempted in fully recrystallized
austenite matrix by precipitation hardening. In case of Ni containing
LDS alloys, a novel strategy of dual precipitation of
-carbides and Ni
rich B2 precipitates showed pathways to obtain higher strength-ductility
combination [31, 32]. These studies have opened up a new possibility of
property enhancement in lightweight induced plasticity (L-IP) steels by
combination of multiple strengthening mechanisms such as shear
band/microband induced plasticity, dynamic slip band refinement,
deformation induced precipitation hardening apart from conventional
precipitation hardening by either
-carbide and/or B2 phase. However,
the role of alloy composition and corresponding hardening/softening
mechanism haven't yet been reported. The present study delved into
fulfill this gap by highlighting the role of alloy composition vis.
vis the
heat treatment temperature on these mechanisms. The synergistic ef-
fects of Mn and Ni on concurrent precipitation and recrystallization have
been addressed in the following sections.
well as subsequent heat treatment and eventually to evaluate their
mechanical behavior in both cold rolled and heat-treated conditions. All
the three alloys could be rolled successfully without any cracking at
room temperature till
reduction in thickness. As anticipated, there
was an increased hardness, but at a differential work hardening rate in
all the three alloys with the degree of cold reduction. The strength of
cold rolled alloys increased significantly to
GPa but at the
expense of their ductility. This is very much expected in metallic ma-
terials, as there can be an increased dislocation density with the amount
of cold working and thereby, enhanced strengthening owing to Taylor
hardening
. It was also observed that rate of increase in
hardness with progress of deformation after
reduction in thickness
decreased significantly. The hardness of all the three alloys at
reduction in thickness was almost similar. This differential work hard-
ening rate with cold work as a function of alloy composition could
entirely be related to the prior microstructural features such as austenite
grain size and B2 phase volume fraction present in the respective alloys.
In our recent study [21] it was noted the selected alloys in hot rolled
condition exhibited about
of total tensile elongation to failure
at room temperature, where 15Mn5Ni alloy displayed lowest ductility.
This suggested that there will be an increased tendency for cracking
when the amount of cold rolling exceeds
reduction in thickness.
Furthermore, the recent literature [22,23] indicated that when the cold
reduction is beyond
the austenitic LDS alloys undergo severe shear
bands formation, which can affect the mechanical properties adversely.
Hence, the cold rolling was restricted to
of reduction in thickness
for all the three alloys for obtaining a better comparison of the micro-
structural evolution in the chosen alloys for a given degree of cold work
and subsequently to evaluate their mechanical properties.
Recrystallization in metallic alloys is affected by the presence of
secondary phase in two ways depending on size of the second phase.
Coarse second phase particles are known to promote particle simulated

properties

B. Mishra et al.
It was well established that LDS alloys containing Al
(in wt.
and
(in wt
) exhibit
-carbides precipitation at elevated
temperatures [12,34]. Size and dissolution of
-carbides, however,
depend upon alloying and ageing temperature-time combination. In the
present study, relatively coarser
-carbides precipitation was observed
within the deformed austenite regions, which was consistent with the
results of Zhang et al. [17] and Brasche et al. [19]. It was suggested that
these
-carbides form by spinodal decomposition during quenching [35,
36]. It was noted that lowering Mn content in presence of Ni has pro-
nounced effect on coarsening of
-carbides than just Ni addition in high
Mn LDS. Dual precipitation of
-carbides and B2 was also observed in
28Mn5Ni and 15Mn5Ni alloys and is consistent with the earlier study
[32]. Nickel rich B2 is known to favor increased rejection of carbon in
the neighborhood austenite and thereby facilitates
-carbides formation
in Ni added LDS as compared to Ni-free LDS [32]. It seems that 28Mn5Ni
alloy has higher austenite stability and lesser B2 precipitation and thus,
limiting rejection of carbon around B2. Very fine
-carbides precipita-
tion was observed within recrystallized grains and is agreement with
literature [29,30,37,38]. These precipitates promote glide plane soft-
ening and accordingly, higher rate of strain hardening [29,30,39].

It was observed that with increase in heat treatment temperature
from

drastically from

in 15Mn5Ni alloy, while its fraction
was too reduced but slightly in the other two alloys. It is to be noted that
the total volume fraction of B2 consisted of three different types include
stringer, intragranular and intergranular. It is important to note that the
volume fraction was calculated by including type-1 and type-3 B2 par-
ticles characterized at EBSD scale and was found to be much higher in
15Mn5Ni alloy. However, there was high volume fraction of type-2 i.e.,
fine intragranular B2 particles, noticed even in 28Mn5Ni alloy at TEM
scale. Dissolution rate of fine intragranular as well as intergranular B2
was noted to be higher than that of B2 stringers. This resulted into
substantial decrease in volume fraction of B2 in 15Mn5Ni alloy. The
presence of higher Mn in 28Mn5Ni alloy led to less B2 precipitation at all
heat treatment temperatures. The high rate of reduction in volume
fraction of B2 in 15Mn5Ni alloy with increase in temperature resulted in
reversal in recrystallization volume fraction of austenite at

noted in Figs. 5a and 28Mn5Ni alloy showed lesser recrystallization

and 28Mn5Ni alloys up to
This indicates pronounced concurrent

inferred that in presence of secondary phase the recrystallization phe-

effect [33]. In the present alloy systems, stringer B2 can act as PSN site
and aids in recrystallization in Ni-containing alloys. The finer inter-
granular/intragranular
-carbide precipitation could occur concur-
rently during heat treatment. The size of the precipitate changes during
thermal treatment and thereby affects the recrystallization in a complex
way. The competing processes of concurrent
-carbide precipitation
and recrystallization kinetics can have profound effect on final micro-
structure, which in turn depends on the alloy composition and heat
treatment temperature/time. Figs. 3, 4 and 6 clearly revealed that
nucleation of recrystallized grains occurs mostly along the
bound-
aries and
interface. It can be inferred that the intragranular B2
particles will not pin the newly recrystallized grains rather may delay
the overall recovery process.
In view of accelerated recrystallization kinetics at and above
where the recrystallization fraction was
in Ni-free LDS leading to
significant drop in strength, Ni addition was explored to facilitate Ni rich
B2 formation and thereby to control recrystallization at higher tem-
peratures. This idea was derived primarily from literature, for example,
Ni addition was first suggested by Kim et al. [5] that resulted into
remarkable increase in strength without much loss in ductility
in Fe-15Mn–9Al–5Ni alloy. Higher strength was attributed to
non-shearable Ni-rich B2 particles. In our recent study [21] it was noted
that B2 in stringer morphology is the limiting factor for ductility. In the
present study too, it was observed that upon heat treatment at 973 K and
1023 K, 15Mn5Ni alloy displayed very low ductility
. This could
be entirely related to the presence of large volume fraction of B2 par-
ticles and co-precipitation of coarser
-carbides and B2 along grain
boundaries. Thus, the processing temperature window for 15Mn5Ni
alloy was found to be around
which can yield optimum com-
bination of strength and ductility.
It has been well established that heat treatment in the two-phase
region controls grain growth in most metallic systems because of
reduced grain boundary migration and mobility due to decreased dif-
fusivities. This is the case in the present study as well. For example, as
can be noted in Fig. 4b,d,f and Fig. 5b that when the alloys were heat
treated at 1173 K significant austenite grain growth was noticed in
28MnoNi alloy (B2-free alloy), but not in the other two alloys containing
secondary B2 phase. This clearly suggests that heat treatment in the two-
phase region helps in attaining refined grain structure in LDs alloys as
well.
Recent studies have indicated that Ni addition in higher Mn con-
taining LDS alloy i.e., Fe-29Mn–9Al-1.2C–6Ni can result into better
strength-ductility combination by heat treatment at
[32]. The properties were found to be superior to the earlier Ni con-
taining LDS at lower Mn levels. In the present study, unlike in
alloy, there was just
recrystallization in 28Mn5Ni alloy. Dual pre-
cipitation of intragranular B2, intergranular B2 and
-carbides along
with partially recrystallized structure led to better strength (1.3–1.6
GPa) and ductility
combination in 28Mn5Ni alloy. With further
increase in the heat treatment temperature to
there was
It is well-known that Mn and C are austenite stabilizers, whereas Al is
a ferrite stabilizer in steels. Though Ni is known to stabilize austenite,
the high (negative) enthalpy of formation between Ni and Al can result
into formation of Ni rich B2 particles during heat treatment. The absence
of Ni enhanced recrystallization and grain growth kinetics in 28MnoNi
alloy significantly, whereas concurrent B2 precipitation in 28Mn5Ni
alloy delayed recrystallization of austenite due to change in diffusion
characteristics and boundary migration. At similar Ni levels, change in
Mn content (28Mn5Ni and 15Mn5Ni) affected stability of austenite
grain structure as well as composition and volume fraction of B2 phase,
and thus affecting solubility of precipitates and recrystallization of
austenite.

5. Conclusions
References
Declaration of competing interest
Acknowledgments
B. Mishra et al.
Credit author statement
Data availability
It is noted that the higher volume fraction of deformed austenite
grains along with
-carbides were present in low temperature heat
treated sample as compared to that observed in the samples heat treated
at 1173 K (Figs. 6 and 7), and this can alter the deformation mecha-
nisms. High strain hardening ability of fully recrystallized LDs alloys
could be associated with the planar slip activity followed by dynamic
slip band refinement process (Figs. 11 and 12). Similar observations
pertaining to high strain hardening rate and associated slip band
refinement were also shown in various earlier studies [5–8]. In addition,
dislocation-twin interaction associated with dynamic slip band refine-
ment was found to be the dominant strain hardening mechanism in
recrystallized alloys. Lower strain hardening at
in 28Mn0Ni alloy
can be attributed to higher volume fraction of deformed austenite region
containing coarse
-carbides. As noticed from TEM examination (Figs. 6
and 7) that the austenite grains contained inhomogeneous distribution
of
-carbides and B2 particles in heat-treated conditions. The presence of
these particles and their uneven size distribution can influence the
change of deformation mechanism from planar glide to wavy slip
involving dislocation cross slip (at local scale) owing to the intrinsic high
SFE of the alloys. Further, dislocation substructure evolution in these
regions suggested that dynamic slip band refinement may be impeded in
such deformed grains at local scale. Moreover, the limited ductility in
low temperature heat treated samples of Ni containing LDs alloys can be
related not only to the reduced strain hardening ability but also to
crack-initiation controlled second phase particles presence.
Authors are grateful to the Director, DMRL for his encouragement
and giving permission for publishing this work and acknowledge DRDO
for funding. Authors would like to acknowledge the members of various
research groups such as Special Melting Group (SMG), Rolling Forging
Group (RFG) and Armour Technology Center (ATC) in DMRL for their
help rendered in preparation of the steel grades.
substantial drop in strength
with
corresponding increase in ductility
in 28Mn5Ni alloy. This could
be due to change in size of B2 precipitates and concurrent changes in
recrystallization kinetics. Thus, the thermo-mechanical processing
window to attain better strength-ductility combination in 28Mn5Ni
alloy should involve tailoring microstructure so as to contain fine
nanosized precipitates.

Three different low-density steels, namely Fe–28Mn–9Al-0.9C
(28Mn0Ni), Fe–28Mn–9Al-0.9C–5Ni (28Mn5Ni) and Fe–15Mn–9Al-
0.9C–5Ni (15Mn5Ni) were used in the present study to understand the
role of concurrent microstructural changes and their effect on thermo-
mechanical processing window. The role of
(ratio) on dual pre-
cipitation of
-carbides as well as B2 phase, which in turn their interplay
with dislocation induced hardening/softening were analyzed. The alloy
composition was chosen such a way that there is a varying B2 phase
fraction from 0 to
in hot rolled condition. The significant findings
of the present study are summarized as follows:

Data will be made available on request.
• Ni addition in high Mn LDS (28Mn5Ni alloy) delayed recrystalliza-
tion significantly. Furthermore, Ni addition along with thermo-
mechanical processing were found to be promising due to concur-
rent fine precipitates of x-carbides as well as Ni rich B2 phase in two-
phase LDS. This facilitates wider processing window for optimizing
the mechanical properties.
• Mechanical property enhancement in Ni-free LDS (28MnoNi alloy)
by thermo-mechanical processing is controlled by dissolution of
-carbides. At temperatures less than the dissolution temperature of
-carbides, deformed zone containing
-carbides has led to the
strength enhancement but limited ductility. A slight heat treatment
temperature rise by
has affected the recrystallization fraction of
austenite matrix grains significantly and accordingly, influenced the
strength-ductility combination in Ni-free LDS.
• Upon cold rolling up to
reduction in thickness, the austenite
grains in all three alloys deformed mostly by planar slip as reflected
by parallel slip bands, while the prior B2 stringers too underwent
concurrent progressive microstructural changes ranging from dislo-
cation accumulation, substructure development and fragmentation.
As anticipated, the mechanical strength and hardness of these alloys
increased with the degree of cold reduction at the expense of their
ductility. All the three alloys attained similar strength levels after
cold reduction with the maximum rate of work hardening in a
single-phase LDS i.e., 28MnONi alloy.
• Subsequent heat treatment after
cold rolling resulted in sub-
stantial microstructural changes such as recrystallization and grain
growth phenomena in austenite along with precipitation and disso-
lution of secondary phases (k-carbides and B2 phase) depending on
the temperature and composition of the alloy. For a given heat
treatment condition i.e., at and above
28Mn0Ni alloy un-
derwent higher rate of recrystallization and grain coarsening in
conjunction with increased twin fraction than that of the other two
Ni-containing alloys.
• Ni containing lower Mn LDS (15Mn5Ni alloy) had detrimental effect
of larger volume fraction of B2 (including prior B2-stringers) and
-carbides precipitation under low heat treatment temperatures of
(cid:)
and 1023 K i.e., in two-phase region. Upon heat treatment at
higher temperature of 1173 K, complete dissolution of
-carbides and
partial dissolution of B2 led to sudden rise in recrystallization frac-
tion of austenite grains. Similar strength-ductility levels along with
improved strain hardening characteristics were noticed in both Ni
containing alloys upon high temperature heat treatment at
